data.title: In HelloNation, Real Estate Experts Pam and Barry Explain Buying or Selling in the Military Market
data.description:
In HelloNation, Real Estate Experts Pam and Barry Explain Buying or Selling in the Military Market

In HelloNation, Real Estate Experts Pam and Barry Explain Buying or Selling in the Military Market

PR Newswire

LAWTON, Okla., March 30, 2026

The article outlines how PCS timelines, VA loans, and local demand shape real estate decisions near military installations.

LAWTON, Okla., March 30, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- What makes buying or selling in the military market different from a traditional real estate transaction? A HelloNation article provides the answer, offering practical insights into the fast-moving conditions that define housing decisions near Fort Sill.

The HelloNation article explains that the military market operates on strict timelines driven by Permanent Change of Station orders. These deadlines leave little room for delay, requiring both buyers and sellers to act quickly and stay organized. The article notes that this urgency shapes every step of the transaction, from home searches to closing schedules, especially in areas like Lawton, where military activity is constant.

The article describes how VA loans play a central role in the military market. These loans allow qualified buyers to purchase homes with no down payment and without private mortgage insurance. This financing option is especially important for military families managing relocation costs. The article explains that VA loans often make homeownership more accessible during a PCS move, when expenses such as travel and temporary housing are already high.

Another key factor covered in the article is the Certificate of Eligibility required for VA loans. The article notes that this document confirms a buyer's qualification and should be secured early in the process. Real Estate Experts Pam and Barry emphasize in the article that delays in obtaining this certificate can slow down transactions already on tight timelines.

The HelloNation article also explains the importance of the VA appraisal. Unlike standard appraisals, this process includes stricter property condition requirements. The article notes that sellers in the military market must be prepared to address potential repairs before closing. Real Estate Experts Pam and Barry highlight that early preparation helps prevent delays and keeps transactions moving forward.

Seller-paid closing costs are another common element discussed in the article. The article explains that many military buyers request these concessions due to the financial strain of relocation. Sellers who understand this expectation can better position their homes in the military market and avoid surprises during negotiations.

Location is also a major consideration. The article notes that proximity to Fort Sill is a top priority for many buyers. Shorter commutes and convenient access to the base influence demand and resale value. The HelloNation article explains that this consistent demand helps shape pricing trends and neighborhood popularity in Lawton real estate.

The article also highlights the role of agents with experience in military relocation. These professionals understand the pace and requirements of the military market, including VA financing and PCS schedules. Real Estate Experts Pam and Barry are featured in the article as professionals familiar with these challenges, helping clients navigate both speed and long-term planning.

In addition, the article addresses alternative options such as renting. The article notes that some families choose to rent due to uncertain assignment lengths or future moves. This flexibility can be useful in a market where long-term plans may change quickly.

The article further explains how remote buying has become common in the military market. Since many buyers cannot visit properties in person, sellers are encouraged to provide detailed listings, high-quality photos, and virtual tours. These tools help buyers make informed decisions while managing relocation logistics remotely.

The article concludes that success in the military market depends on preparation, timing, and understanding key factors like VA loans, appraisals, and relocation schedules. Buyers and sellers who understand these elements can move through the process with greater confidence and fewer delays.

Buying or Selling in the Military Market features insights from Pam and Barry, Real Estate Experts of Lawton, Oklahoma, in HelloNation.

About HelloNation
HelloNation is a premier media platform that connects readers with trusted professionals and businesses across various industries. Through its innovative "edvertising" approach that blends educational content with storytelling, HelloNation delivers expert-driven, good-news articles that inform, inspire, and empower. Covering topics from home improvement and health to business strategy and lifestyle, HelloNation highlights leaders making a meaningful impact in their communities.

SOURCE HelloNation

data.title: Statement from Shelley McNeil, President of CUPE Local 5047 – Halifax Regional Centre for Education
data.description:
Statement from Shelley McNeil, President of CUPE Local 5047 – Halifax Regional Centre for Education

Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE) Local 5047 members—school librarians working in the Halifax Regional Centre for Education—keep literacy alive in our schools. They support students who are struggling, they create spaces where curiosity and belonging grow, and they make sure young people see themselves reflected in what they read.

The services our library staff provide are not optional. They are foundational. Right now, they are being treated as expendable.

Over the past few weeks, we have been watching a funding dispute unfold between City Council and the School Board, and in the middle of it are our members and the students they serve—being used as pawns in a political standoff. This is unacceptable.

The School Board must clearly and publicly affirm that school library programs will continue, and that the workers who deliver them will not lose their jobs.

City Councillors must ensure that stable, ongoing funding is in place so that these programs are not repeatedly thrown into uncertainty. Pulling the rug out from under essential services—and the people who provide them—is not responsible governance. It is a failure of leadership.

Our members deserve stability. Students deserve access to the resources that help them learn, grow, and succeed. And the public deserves institutions that act with clarity, accountability, and care.

We are calling on both the Halifax Regional Centre for Education and Halifax Regional Municipality City Councillors to do their jobs: protect these programs, protect these workers, and put students first.

mdj:so/cope491

Media contact:
Mary-Dan Johnston
CUPE Atlantic Region Communications
mjohnston@cupe.ca

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260330656711/en/

data.title: Texas Healthcare and Bioscience Institute Releases Strategic Vision to Advance the Texas Life Sciences Ecosystem
data.description:
Texas Healthcare and Bioscience Institute Releases Strategic Vision to Advance the Texas Life Sciences Ecosystem

Texas Healthcare and Bioscience Institute Releases Strategic Vision to Advance the Texas Life Sciences Ecosystem

PR Newswire

AUSTIN, Texas, March 30, 2026

AUSTIN, Texas, March 30, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- The Texas Healthcare & Bioscience Institute (THBI), the statewide public policy voice of the healthcare and bioscience industry, today released a comprehensive report outlining a strategic vision for strengthening Texas' life sciences and biotechnology ecosystem. "Texas Life Sciences: Driving Innovation, Patient Care & Economic Growth" highlights the state's rapid growth in bioscience employment, research investment, and innovation while offering actionable recommendations to sustain long-term leadership in the industry.

 Hosted by THBI, Texas Life Sciences Summit kicks off Tuesday, March 31 and runs through April 1 in Austin, Texas.

Texas has emerged as one of the nation's leading bioscience hubs, leveraging its extensive research infrastructure, expanding workforce, and business-friendly environment. According to the report, the state's bioscience industry employed 129,245 individuals across 9,212 establishments in 2023, representing 21.6% employment growth since 2019.

The report highlights the scale and momentum of the Texas bioscience sector:

  • $4.6 billion in bioscience-related academic research and development expenditures in 2022, ranking third nationally

  • $1.8 billion in NIH funding awarded to Texas institutions in 2023, a 34% increase since 2019

  • $8.7 billion in venture capital investment raised by Texas bioscience companies between 2019 and 2023, ranking fifth nationally

  • Nearly 8,000 bioscience-related patents attributed to Texas inventors between 2019 and 2023, ranking ninth nationally

The report also outlines several policy and ecosystem recommendations designed to strengthen Texas' leadership in life sciences and biotechnology, and calls for deeper collaboration between government, academia, and industry to modernize research infrastructure, accelerate commercialization of discoveries, and expand access to emerging technologies.

As the statewide organization representing the life science and biotechnology industry, THBI aims to use this report as a roadmap for policymakers, industry leaders, researchers, and educators to collaborate in strengthening Texas' global competitiveness. By fostering innovation, supporting workforce development, and enhancing research and manufacturing capacity, Texas can continue to drive advancements in healthcare, biotechnology, and economic growth for decades to come.

The full report can be found at https://www.thbi.com/industry-reports.

The report is being released in conjunction with the annual Texas Life Sciences Summit, hosted by the Texas Healthcare and Bioscience Institute, which kicks off Tuesday, March 31 and runs through April 1 in Austin. The Summit convenes leaders from across the healthcare, biotechnology, research, and policy communities to explore the trends, investments, and innovations shaping the future of life sciences in Texas. The report serves as a timely foundation for these discussions, offering data-driven insights and strategic recommendations to inform collaboration and accelerate growth across the state's ecosystem. Media wishing to attend the Summit should contact media@crosswindpr.com.

About the Texas Healthcare and Bioscience Institute
The Texas Healthcare and Bioscience Institute (THBI) is the Texas public policy voice for the healthcare and bioscience industry. The Texas life sciences industry is committed to expanding the boundaries of science by discovering, developing, and delivering innovative and needed medications to patients. It is the patient that is the ultimate beneficiary of such advances. The membership of THBI is dedicated to creating an environment where such discoveries flourish and thrive. For more information on THBI and upcoming events celebrating its 30th anniversary, please visit www.thbi.com or contact info@thbi.com.

Media Contact:
Thomas Graham
CEO | Crosswind
media@crosswindpr.com
512-537-1414

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/texas-healthcare-and-bioscience-institute-releases-strategic-vision-to-advance-the-texas-life-sciences-ecosystem-302728946.html

SOURCE Texas Healthcare and Bioscience Institute

data.title: 'Several hurricanes have come close': I'm 73 and live in a mobile home in Florida. Do I ditch my $2,400 home insurance?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I live in a 2016 double-wide mobile home with no mortgage'

"My home insurance continues to rise and the amount covered would never fully replace my home." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I am a 73-year-old single woman who lives 10 miles from the ocean in the western Gulf area of Florida. I live in a 2016 double-wide mobile home with no mortgage. Over the past few years, several hurricanes have come close, but luckily, I haven't had any damage.

I pay $2,400 per year, which includes a $5,000 deductible and covers the total amount. They note that additional coverage for contents, liability and similar protections could likely be added if needed. Surprisingly, I am one of the few people in their park who actually has insurance.

My home insurance continues to rise and the amount covered would never fully replace my home. I also know that I would not return if my home were destroyed. Is it worth paying for insurance every year, or should I end my coverage and deal with the potential outcome?

Thank you for your advice.

Homeowner

Don't miss: 'I want safe returns': I'm 73 with $300,000 saved. I'm not interested in the stock market. What should I do?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Senior housing would be a good option in your case, but competition is tough.

Dear Homeowner,

Florida is featuring quite prominently in this column of late.

You have the answer in your back pocket. If losing your home and its contents - lest we forget about them - would cause you financial distress, don't get rid of your home insurance. It would help you get back on your feet if you have little other savings. If you do have substantial savings, enough to start again, rent/buy a little place elsewhere as a bit of self-insurance.

Your premiums are in line with the average homeowner, but you are still at the higher end. The average American pays nearly $2,500 per year for home insurance. That's up nearly 10% over the last three years. (That doesn't include property tax, which can range from $1,500 a year to more than $7,000, depending on where you live and the size of your property.)

What's missing from your letter is the actual value of your mobile home. A double-wide mobile home purchased 10 years ago probably cost $75,000 to $100,000, depending on the type and size. Today, it could cost up to $160,000. Without a mortgage, you have no lender requiring insurance, so you remain personally responsible for all losses related to your home.

That not only includes damage to your home but also liability claims if someone (including guests) is injured on your property. Whatever you do, don't go without liability insurance. Owning your home outright does not eliminate these risks. Going without insurance only makes sense if you have sufficient liquid assets to cover a worst-case scenario and move on.

Some Moneyist Facebook Group members are sympathetic. "Mobile homes are 'depreciating' assets. So the value of the structure is actually decreasing every year," one man wrote. "This makes insuring a mobile home somewhat optional. Other than liability and contents coverage, you might consider just having a reserve fund of what you estimate it would cost to replace."

Others are more circumspect. "If there were a hurricane and it was destroyed, how much would it cost you to set up someplace?" a woman writes. "With a $5,000 deductible, do you have an emergency fund of two times that to deal with a new place, replacements and clean up? If there was a fire, same question, but different insurance fine print. Have a good Plan B."

Related: 'Selling will be a very difficult process': My mom, 93, owns a timeshare in Florida. How can I disclaim this inheritance?

Why people 'self-insure'

A little background: Roughly one in seven owner-occupied homes in the U.S is not insured. It's partly because some homeowners, like yourself, say they can't afford the rising premiums due to documented climate change and extreme weather conditions, the cost of rebuilding and/or replacing a home, and the additional costs required for flood insurance.

Homeowners usually choose to "self-insure" - not exactly the most encouraging of terms - when they own their home outright and would not rebuild, as in this case, when insurance costs feel high compared with the potential benefit, as you feel, and when they are financially able to handle a loss without serious hardship. This remains the great unknown in your case.

Before you make a decision, you can try to negotiate your homeowner's insurance, both to reduce premiums before a claim and to improve settlements after a loss. Ask potential insurers about discounts for security systems, smoke detectors, energy rating, having a long-term policy without any payments and/or even being a nonsmoker.

Bundling home and auto policies with the same provider can, in some cases, lead to savings of up to 20%. Increasing your deductible may also lower your premiums (better to pay a higher deductible in return for a lower premium than be saddled with a $250,000 bill for rebuilding). In five years, you could save $12,000, or face a massive rebuilding cost.

Senior housing would be a good option in your case, but competition is tough. The senior housing market faces a deficit of 500,000 to 900,000 new units by the end of the decade, depending on the source. Millions of low-income seniors are struggling with the high cost of homeownership and insurance, like you, and the seemingly never-ending burden of rising rents.

The Treasury Department found in a report released last year that the majority of nonrenewals are related to increased costs associated with climate change. Nonrenewal rates were about 80% higher in areas facing the greatest risks from climate-related disasters. Climate change is also raising costs for insurance companies, as payouts are increasing in those areas.

It's a high-risk choice and, for that reason, it's yours alone.

Related: 'This is a first-world problem': I can't roll over my $800,000 401(k) from my prior employer. What did I do wrong?

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'We're living the simple life': I was a fisherman and my wife was a nurse. We retired with $6 million. Here's how we did it.

My PayPal account received money from the Philippines with two phone numbers listed. I called them. Big mistake.

'The bank told me I could be liable': I found out why my brother, who has a reverse mortgage, ran out of money

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  03-30-26 1354ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: CVS to Grow Store Footprint This Year With 60 New Locations — Update
data.description:
By Connor Hart 




  CVS Health is planning to open about 60 stores in 2026, growing its footprint for the first time after years of contraction. 



  The openings will include traditional stores, locations within Target stores and pharmacy-only sites, spokeswoman Amy Thibault said Monday. 



  CVS has a small number of closures planned for the year, primarily due to lease expirations and relocations, she added. 



  "While we regularly review our footprint, we've completed work to optimize our retail footprint to best serve the needs of our patients and customers," the company said. 



  CVS over the past few years has undergone a significant downsizing of its footprint. The chain, which announced plans in 2021 to reduce its store density, said it would close about 270 locations in 2025, following roughly 800 net closures over the prior three years, The Wall Street Journal previously reported. 



  National drugstore chains including CVS have struggled in recent years to address both competition from discount retailers and a surge in theft, the Journal has said. The challenges have led to cost-cutting, widespread store closures and other dramatic changes at these companies. 



  CVS has sought to address these challenges in part by launching new store formats, including its pharmacy-only sites. The company last year opened about a dozen of these locations, which are less than half the size of typical CVS location. 



  The shrunken CVS stores stock health-related products, such as over-the-counter cough and pain medications, but they don't carry the vast array of consumer items that for decades have been staples at its stores, the Journal previously reported. 





  Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  March 30, 2026 13:37 ET (17:37 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
